questions.question: An individual who is not a member of either House of Parliament may be appointed to the Council of Ministers; however, they must become a member of either House within a specified time frame of:
questions.solution: An individual not initially a member of either House of Parliament can be appointed as a Minister. Constitution of India mandates such a person must get elected or nominated to either House within 6 months. If the person fails to become a member within 6 months, they cease to be a Minister. Therefore, the specified time frame is 6 months, i.e., Option 3. This is as per Article 75(5) of the Indian Constitution. Hence, the correct answer is Option 3.

questions.question: Article 23 of the Indian Constitution prohibits which of the following?
questions.option_1: Prohibition of discrimination on grounds of religion, race, caste, sex or place of birth
questions.option_2: Prohibition of solemnisation of child marriages
questions.option_3: Prohibition of traffic in human beings and forced labour
questions.option_4: Prohibition of employment of children in factories, etc.
questions.solution: Correct option is 3. Article 23 of the Indian Constitution prohibits trafficking in human beings and forced labour. It is one of the Fundamental Rights under the chapter on Right against Exploitation. This article ensures no person is subjected to trafficking or forced labour. Other options relate to different laws or articles. Hence, the answer is option 3. This completes the answer.

questions.question: In 1964, who chaired the Committee on Prevention of Corruption and suggested the Central Vigilance Commission be established?
questions.solution: Correct option is 3: K. Santhanam. K. Santhanam chaired the Committee on Prevention of Corruption in 1964. The committee suggested the establishment of the Central Vigilance Commission. This was a key step in setting up an institutional mechanism to prevent corruption in government. Thus, the Chairperson of this committee was K. Santhanam. Final answer: Option 3

questions.question: In which year was the first national Census of India carried out during British India?
questions.solution: The first national Census of India under British rule was carried out in 1901. Earlier censuses were regional or not comprehensive at a national level. Hence, the officially recognised first all-India census year is 1901. Final answer: Option 4

questions.question: Open Market Operations (OMOs) are conducted by RBI to control ____________.
questions.solution: Open Market Operations (OMOs) are tools used by the Reserve Bank of India (RBI). They involve buying and selling government securities in the open market. This influences the liquidity in the banking system and hence controls the money supply. When RBI buys securities, it injects money, increasing money supply. When RBI sells securities, it withdraws money, reducing money supply. Therefore, OMOs are conducted primarily to regulate money supply in the economy. Final answer: Option 2

questions.question: The 44ᵗʰ Amendment Act of 1978 restored the standard term of the Lok Sabha and State Legislature to how many years?
questions.solution: The 44th Amendment Act of 1978 restored the term of the Lok Sabha and State Legislatures to five years. Before the amendment, during emergency, the term was extended up to seven years. This amendment reinstated the original duration defined in the Constitution. Hence, the correct term restored is five years. Final answer: Option 1

questions.question: What is the constitutional consequence if the Lok Sabha passes a no-confidence motion against the ruling government?
questions.option_1: Only the Prime Minister must submit resignation
questions.option_2: The Council of Ministers is obligated to step down
questions.option_3: The Speaker of Lok Sabha is removed by the President
questions.option_4: The government may continue without any change
questions.solution: When the Lok Sabha passes a no-confidence motion, it means the House has expressed lack of support for the ruling government. According to the Constitution of India, this requires the entire Council of Ministers to resign. The government must step down as it no longer enjoys the majority support in the Lok Sabha. Therefore, the Council of Ministers, including the Prime Minister, is obliged to resign after such a motion is passed. Option 2 is correct. Answer complete and verified.

questions.question: What is the correct sequence in which the oath of office binds a Union Minister?
questions.option_1: Integrity → Constitution → Sovereignty → Duty → Impartiality (justice without fear or favour)
questions.option_3: Allegiance to the → Constitution, → Sovereignty and integrity → Faithful discharge of duties→ Impartiality
questions.option_4: Allegiance → Sovereignty → Duty and Impartiality→ Faithful discharge
questions.solution: The oath of office for a Union Minister follows a specific order reflecting key principles. First, there is allegiance to the Constitution, establishing loyalty to the supreme law of India. Next, the Minister swears to uphold sovereignty and integrity of the country. Then comes the promise for faithful discharge of duties, ensuring responsibility and dedication. Finally, it includes impartiality, meaning justice without fear or favour. Hence, Option 3 correctly presents the sequence of the oath of office for Union Ministers. Final answer: Option 3

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Draft National Sports Governance Bill, 2024, introduced by the Indian Government?
questions.option_1: To establish a framework for ethical governance and athlete welfare in sports federations
questions.option_2: To increase commercialization of sports in Urban and Semiurban areas
questions.option_3: To centralize all sports decisions under one authority
questions.option_4: To eliminate traditional sports from national competitions
questions.solution: The Draft National Sports Governance Bill, 2024 aims to improve governance in sports federations. It focuses on ethical management and protecting athlete welfare. The bill provides a legal framework to ensure transparency and accountability in sports bodies. It does not focus on commercialization or centralizing sports decisions. Nor does it aim to remove traditional sports from competitions. Hence, the main purpose aligns with option 1. Final answer: Option 1

questions.question: Which Fundamental Duty was added by the 86ᵗʰ Constitutional Amendment in 2002?
questions.option_1: To provide opportunities for education to children aged 6–14 years
questions.option_2: To promote harmony among all people of India
questions.option_3: To uphold and protect the sovereignty of India
questions.solution: The 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002, added a Fundamental Duty relating to education. It mandates that parents or guardians provide opportunities for education to their children aged 6 to 14 years. This duty supports the Right to Education and promotes attendance in school for children in this age group. Other options are Fundamental Duties but were not introduced by this amendment. Final answer: Option 1

questions.question: Which of the following articles is INCORRECTY matched with the Constitutional Provisions related to Parliament ?
questions.option_1: Article 99 ---Composition of the Council of States/Rajya Sabha
questions.option_2: Article 93 ----Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the House of People
questions.option_3: Article 89---- Chairman and Deputy Chairman of Council of States
questions.option_4: Article 105--- Powers and Privileges of the Houses of Parliament
questions.solution: Article 99 of the Indian Constitution deals with the 'Oath or affirmation by Members of Parliament', not the composition of the Council of States (Rajya Sabha). The composition of the Rajya Sabha is specified under Article 80. Article 93 relates to the Speaker and Deputy Speaker of the House of the People (Lok Sabha). Article 89 deals with the Chairman and Deputy Chairman of the Council of States (Rajya Sabha). Article 105 defines the Powers and Privileges of the Houses of Parliament. Thus, only Option 1 incorrectly matches the article with the constitutional provision related to Parliament. Final answer: Option 1

questions.question: Which of the following bodies is described as the "real executive" in the Indian political system?
questions.solution: Solution: President is the nominal executive . Prime Minister actually runs the government and exercises executive powers . Therefore, the Prime Minister is called the real executive in the Indian political system.

questions.question: Which of the following objectives is stated in Article 38 of the Indian Constitution?
questions.option_1: To establish a social order based on justice—social, economic, and political
questions.option_2: To guarantee employment to all citizens
questions.option_3: To provide equal education opportunities for all
questions.option_4: To establish military rule for national security
questions.solution: Article 38 of the Indian Constitution directs the State to strive to promote the welfare of the people by securing a social order characterised by justice—social, economic, and political. This is a Directive Principle of State Policy guiding the government to create conditions of equality and fairness. Options 2, 3, and 4 are not stated in Article 38. Thus, the correct objective stated in Article 38 is to establish a social order based on justice—social, economic, and political. Final answer: Option 1

questions.question: Which of the following provisions in the Indian Constitution deals with the duty of the State to separate the judiciary from the executive?
questions.solution: The duty of the State to separate the judiciary from the executive is specifically mentioned in Article 50 of the Indian Constitution. Article 50 directs the State to take steps to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State. This provision ensures the independence of the judiciary, which is a basic feature of the Constitution. Article 44 relates to Uniform Civil Code, Article 48A deals with protecting the environment, and Article 51 talks about promotion of international peace and security. Therefore, the correct provision is Article 50. Final answer: Option 1

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Parliamentary Committees is TRUE?
questions.option_1: There are no temporary Parliamentary Committees.
questions.option_3: Ad Hoc Committees are permanent in nature.
questions.option_4: Standing Committees are reconstituted from time to time and are permanent.
questions.solution: Standing Committees of Parliament are permanent and are reconstituted periodically, usually annually. These Committees handle ongoing responsibilities and scrutinise bills, budgets, and government functions. Ad Hoc Committees are temporary and formed for specific tasks; they are not permanent. Therefore, statement 4 is true as it correctly describes Standing Committees. Statements 1, 2, and 3 are false as there are both permanent and temporary committees, and Ad Hoc Committees are not permanent. Hence, the correct option is 4. Final answer: Option 4

questions.question: Which of the following statements correctly differentiates the power of the Rajya Sabha from the Lok Sabha?
questions.option_1: Rajya Sabha can initiate a motion of no-confidence against the Council of Ministers
questions.option_2: Rajya Sabha can authorise Parliament to make laws on State subjects
questions.option_3: Rajya Sabha has equal power with Lok Sabha in all financial matters
questions.option_4: Rajya Sabha can amend Money Bills
questions.solution: Option 2 is correct as per the Indian Constitution. The Rajya Sabha can authorise Parliament to make laws on State subjects under Article 249. This power is unique to Rajya Sabha, Lok Sabha cannot do this alone. Option 1 is incorrect because only Lok Sabha can initiate a motion of no-confidence against the Council of Ministers. Option 3 is incorrect because Rajya Sabha has limited powers in financial matters; Money Bills are primarily controlled by Lok Sabha. Option 4 is incorrect since only Lok Sabha can amend Money Bills, as per Article 109. Hence, the correct differentiation is reflected in Option 2. Final answer: Option 2

questions.question: Who was the Chairman of the 'Committee to Review Policy for Public Enterprises', set up by the Government of India in 1984?
questions.solution: The 'Committee to Review Policy for Public Enterprises' was set up by the Government of India in 1984. Arjun Sengupta was appointed as the Chairman of this committee. This committee played a key role in recommending reforms for public sector enterprises. Hence, the correct answer is Arjun Sengupta.
